query: effectiveness of inositol in treating trichotillomania

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of trichotillomania. Review the presentation of a patient with trichotillomania. Summarize the treatment options for trichotillomania. Explain the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by trichotillomania. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Current research suggests modest positive effects with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors. The effects are more robust in combination with therapy. There have been meta-analysis reviews of more recent research that have shown a moderate positive effect of SSRI medications, but a more pronounced effect was seen with therapy. [11] There are more recent preliminary data that have shown some positive effects with olanzapine, aripiprazole, and quetiapine. [12] [13] [14] [25] The studies with antipsychotics are few and require future studies to replicate results. Past studies using clomipramine and others in the tricyclic antidepressant medication class have been studied to treat TTM and efficacy has been found with more research supporting clomipramine. [15] Other novel treatment case studies using N-acetylcysteine have shown positive results, but there have been no robust studies. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Trichotillomania (TTM) also known as hair-pulling disorder, was first described in ancient Greece, but its current name was coined in the later part of the 18th century. In these cases the hair is pulled from anywhere on the body repeatedly, appearing as hair loss but is caused by the action of the patient. As this condition can greatly affect the appearance of the patient, it is associated with societal stigma. This activity describes the etiology, presentation, and management of trichotillomania and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Studies focusing on neurochemistry have shown a relationship with the serotonin 2A receptor. [7] Most studies that focus on neurotransmitters or neurochemistry are based on the patient's response to therapy that modulates these neurotransmitters. For example, multiple randomized controlled studies look at treatment with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). Some have yielded positive results, and some have been equivocal. [7] [8] [9] [10] One meta-analysis that looked at SSRIs showed moderate effects on patient symptoms, compared to more sizeable effects of behavior therapy. [11] Other studies suggest that the dopamine system is also involved. There have been double-blind, placebo-controlled trials that have shown positive effects with olanzapine, and the reversal of TTM has been shown with aripiprazole treatment. [12] [13] [14] There have been studies that have shown clomipramine also can be effective. [15] Generally, these studies suggest the involvement of the monoamine system with serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine receptors. Data in these studies are often not robust and need to be replicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There is a significant stigma surrounding self-inflicted pathological hair loss thus patients may be hesitant to discuss it. Remember to keep a high degree of suspicion. Often, an interprofessional approach to treatment and management will include, dermatology, psychiatry, and psychology.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, the disorder has no cure and all treatments have limitations. The disorder has relapses and remissions. In the long run, the patient has permanent loss of hair, scarring, and poor cosmesis. [Level 5] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be encouraged to avoid stressful situations and triggers for their hair-pulling behavior. Certain activities are more likely to be associated with hair-pulling in TTM and include driving, reading or doing paperwork, watching television, and talking on the phone. Patients should be advised to limit these activities when possible and to increase their amount of physical activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Trichotillomania is a multifaceted disorder that often involves various specialties and cross-specialties as well as multiple treatment modalities. The patient may be seen by a primary care clinician, a dermatologist, a psychiatrist, and a licensed clinical psychologist. The treatment will likely include therapy techniques, and there may be the use of medications. The currently studied therapy techniques for the treatment of trichotillomania include cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and habit reversal training.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Habit reversal training is grounded in CBT techniques and it aims to identify cognitive distortions and thought-action pairings and change them. For example, a patient notes that they have stressful group activities at work and after this, they notice that pulling hair out alleviates this stress. The cognitive distortion/thought of all social interaction creating stress is paired with the hair-pulling as a way to alleviate the stress, and so this behavior is negatively reinforced by the alleviation of the stress, and the connection is strengthened. Habit reversal therapy is a low-risk treatment for TTM that has been shown to be effective. [18] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis is better when the disorder is diagnosed early, and treatment begins early. It is also associated with a better prognosis the younger the age of occurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Psychiatry may be consulted. A dermatology consult may also be sought. Consultation with a licensed therapist is also part of treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other factors in the etiology are neuropsychology and the cognitive components. Many patients with trichotillomania report that there was a stressful situation that occurred before the hair-pulling behavior. Others describe boredom before hair-pulling. [16] These feelings of boredom or stress are negative effects, or internal feelings or emotions, which have been shown in research to correlate to the increased behavior of pulling hair. If we examine the action in behavioral terms, there is a sense of tension that precedes hair-pulling behavior. [16] [17] This tension is then relieved by the hair pulling itself. This creates a cycle of learned and reinforced behavioral activity. The negative effect, or emotion, is paired with the behavior which relieves the negative effect and thus is reinforced and repeated. [7] [8] [18] For example, the patient feels stressed, pulls hair, stress goes away, and thus the alleviation of the stress reinforces the behavior of hair-pulling. Some patients report an association of depressive symptoms likely resulting in some response of TTM to SSRI treatment. Since the psychiatric and psychological symptomatology is diverse, no single predictor can be named at this time. Currently, it is accepted that behavioral therapy is the mainstay of treatment and that the neuropsychology of TTM is the greater of the etiological components.

retrieved_docs.title: Terbinafine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Terbinafine is an antifungal medication that works through the inhibition of squalene epoxidase. It has activity against most dermatophytes, and it has approval for use as an oral therapy for the treatment of onychomycosis. Although most side effects are mild and self-limited, such as headache and gastrointestinal symptoms, taste disturbances (dysgeusia) can range from mild to severe, resulting in weight loss, and have rarely been reported permanent. This activity describes the indications, mode of action, and side effects of terbinafine and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in ensuring proper usage.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Habit reversal therapy has three components: awareness, competing for the response, and social support. [7] The patient is trained to be aware of hair-pulling and of the situations or emotions that cause the hair pulling. They are trained to notice when they are in the act and when they are about to perform the behavior. They are praised for correct awareness and reminded if incorrect. Once this is solidified, then they are taught a competing response which is some action that takes place instead of hair removal. They are to complete this task when they are pulling the hair or if they have the urge to pull. The social support from those around the individual that praise the appropriate use of habit reversal training or remind the patient to use the training when they are not doing so is core to habit reversal training. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Impulse Control Disorders -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Impulse control disorders are pervasive and often life long manifestations of disabling behavioral patterns. Unchecked, these disorders can result in deleterious outcomes for those afflicted. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of impulse control disorders and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.content: Papaverine and its derivatives that are employed in the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders are classified as drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders and are members of the papaverin group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1325
retrieved_docs.content: Isopropamide iodide is a medication that may be employed in the management of الإِسْهال (diarrhea), indicating its potential therapeutic role for this gastrointestinal condition. In contrast, hydroxid hořečnatý (magnesium hydroxide) can induce الإِسْهال, reflecting its known laxative effect as a side‑effect of its antacid and bowel‑cleansing properties. These opposing actions illustrate how agents affecting intestinal motility can either be used to treat diarrhea or, conversely, precipitate it, underscoring the importance of selecting appropriate therapies and monitoring for adverse gastrointestinal outcomes in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1900
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate may be used to treat إِمْساك (constipation), and إِمْساك is clinically associated with تَقَيُّء (vomiting). This relationship indicates that alleviating constipation with magnesium citrate could also impact the occurrence or severity of vomiting, highlighting the interconnected nature of gastrointestinal symptoms and the therapeutic role of magnesium citrate in managing constipation‑related clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1947
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate is a compound that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea). Isopropamide iodide, an anticholinergic medication, is also linked to the management of الإِسْهال, indicating that both bismuth subnitrate and isopropamide iodide can be considered therapeutic options for this gastrointestinal condition. This highlights the availability of multiple pharmacologic strategies for addressing diarrhea in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both therapeutic agents that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب معدي (gastrointestinal disorder). Each of these bismuth compounds shares the same indicated uses, suggesting they have comparable efficacy in managing diarrheal conditions and related gastrointestinal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea) as well as اضطراب مَعِدِيّ (gastrointestinal disorder). Methylcelulosa, which belongs to the demulcent class, may also be employed in the management of الإِسْهال and contains the inactive ingredient terbinafine 250 MG oral tablet. Together, these compounds illustrate multiple pharmacologic options for addressing diarrheal conditions and related gastrointestinal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 549
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat both اضطراب معدي (gastrointestinal disorder) and الإِسْهال (diarrhea). Both bismuth compounds, however, are contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (renal impairment) because of the risk of toxicity in compromised kidney function. Isopropamide iodide is also employed in the management of الإِسْهال, providing an additional option for controlling this symptom. In contrast, hydroxid hořečnatý (magnesium hydroxide) can induce الإِسْهال, reflecting its laxative effect and the need to avoid its use when diarrhea is undesirable. Together, these relationships illustrate the therapeutic roles of bismuth‑containing medications and anticholinergic agents in treating diarrheal and gastrointestinal disorders, while highlighting the importance of avoiding their use in renal impairment and recognizing agents that may exacerbate diarrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2299
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both used therapeutically to manage diarrhea (الإِسْهال) and broader gastrointestinal disturbances (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ). In contrast, magnesium hydroxide (hydroxid hořečnatý) can provoke diarrhea as an adverse effect. Thus, while magnesium hydroxide may induce diarrheal symptoms, the bismuth compounds serve as treatments for those same symptoms and for related gastrointestinal disorders, highlighting their complementary roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 350
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC class “Other drugs for peptic ulcer and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease (GORD)” includes acetoxolone, bismuth subnitrate, bismuth subcitrate, gefarnate and is grouped within the proglumid category. Both bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are reported to treat gastrointestinal disturbances and diarrhea, but they are contraindicated in patients with scleroderma. Isopropamide iodide is used to manage diarrhea, while magnesium hydroxide (hydroxid hořečnatý) can induce diarrhea as a side effect. These connections illustrate that several ulcer‑protective agents also have antidiarrheal effects, necessitating careful selection of therapy in conditions such as scleroderma and awareness of the laxative potential of magnesium‑based compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 423
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat both الإسهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب معيدي (gastrointestinal disorder). In addition, isopropamide iodide is employed in the management of الإسهال (diarrhea). Together, these medications provide options for alleviating acute and chronic disturbances of the gastrointestinal tract, highlighting their relevance in the treatment of diarrheal illnesses and broader gastrointestinal dysfunction.
